title: Gothic textura capitals
subtitle: Lombardic versals and their role alongside the textura minuscule
excerpt: Gothic textura minuscule does not have its own capital forms. The capital letters used with textura are Lombardic versals, a rounded, ornamented letter drawn rather than written. These two hands occupy different scales: the textura body text and the drawn Lombardic at chapter openings and initials.
sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906), chapters on versals and Gothic scripts. Johnston's analysis of the Lombardic versal as the counterpart to textura minuscule is the canonical reference for this pairing. Internet Archive, archive.org/details/writingilluminat00john.

glossaryTerms.definition: A drawn capital letter associated with medieval manuscript illumination and used as a companion capital to Gothic textura minuscule. The versal is built from two or three pen strokes with the hollow body filled with ink, not written in a single pass like a Roman capital.

A compressed, angular blackletter script that dominated Northern European manuscript production from roughly the twelfth to the fifteenth centuries. The dense vertical strokes create a woven visual texture across the page.

content.text: minuscule has no capital forms of its own. Medieval scribes paired it with

s: drawn rounded capitals at a larger scale than the minuscule text, used at chapter openings and initials. The dense compressed textura and the open rounded versal were deliberately contrasted, and this pairing is typical of the period.

A Lombardic versal is not a written letter. It is built from outline strokes that define the letter skeleton, with the body either filled or left open. The broad-edge nib used for textura is also used for the versals, but at a larger scale. The widest Mitchell Roundhand nib (0) or a Brause Bandzug is the right starting point.

items.name: Mitchell Roundhand nib, 0 (slug: nib-mitchell-roundhand-0)
items.description: Broadest nib in the Mitchell Roundhand series. Used for the outline strokes and filling of the versal body.

items.name: Iron-gall ink or sumi ink (slug: iron-gall-ink)
items.description: For practising. Iron-gall for a period-appropriate result; sumi for a deeper black on demonstration pieces.

items.name: Cartridge paper, 220 gsm (slug: cartridge-paper-220)
items.description: Heavyweight cartridge for the finished work. The versal strokes are slow and deliberate; the paper needs to handle repeated pen movements without fluffing.

items.name: Tracing paper (slug: tracing-paper)
items.description: For planning versal proportions before committing to the good sheet.

items.name: Light box (slug: light-box-a4)
items.description: Optional. Useful for tracing a pencil skeleton underneath the good sheet.

content.text: The standard proportions for a Lombardic capital: the letter height is 7-8 nib-widths of the nib used for the textura body text. So if the textura minuscule is written at 4 nib-widths x-height with a Mitchell 3, the Lombardic versal is drawn 7-8 Mitchell-3 nib-widths tall, or equivalently 3-4 nib-widths tall with the Mitchell 0. The versal is drawn, not measured, so these ratios are guides rather than rules.

The letter O sets the base proportion for all Lombardic capitals. It is a nearly circular form: the widest point is roughly equal to the height, slightly compressed. All other capitals derive from this circle: C is three-quarters of the O circle; G is C with a horizontal spur; D is the circle split vertically with a stem on the left.

Stroke 1: the left diagonal hairline, drawn with the very corner of the nib at approximately 45 degrees from the baseline to the cap height. Stroke 2: the right diagonal, matching the left in angle and weight, narrowing at the crossing point. Stroke 3: the horizontal crossbar at mid-height, drawn with the full nib width held at 0 degrees to create a thick horizontal bar. Stroke 4: small serifs applied at the feet of both diagonal strokes with the corner of the nib. Fill the triangular body of the A with a slow careful infill stroke, keeping the edges of the body outline just inside the hairline strokes.
The nib angle for Lombardic versals

Hold the nib at 0 degrees from the baseline for thick horizontal strokes, and at about 30 degrees for diagonal hairlines. The angle shifts during each letter rather than staying fixed as it does in textura. This is the key difference between a drawn letter and a written one. A drawn letter changes the nib angle to control stroke weight. A written letter gets its weight variation from a consistent angle and the direction of movement.

Plan the layout before writing any textura. Mark the position and size of each Lombardic initial on the good sheet in light pencil. Write the textura text around the marked spaces. Draw the versals last, when the surrounding text is complete and you can judge the visual balance. The versal should be large enough to stand clearly at the opening of its word but not so large that it overwhelms the following textura line.
